narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an LSAT score of 32, your academic profile shows solid but not standout performance compared to peers who typically succeed in law school and on the bar exam. Your Year 1 school decile of 3 and Year 3 decile of 4 place you in the middle of the pack at your institution, while your upper-middle family income aligns with many applicants but doesn't stand out as a major factor here. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam outcomes based on profiles like yours, estimated a 40.4% chance that you would fail the bar—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass on their first try and uphold strong bar passage rates. Among all the details in your application, three factors had the strongest sway in this decision: your undergraduate GPA, your Year 3 school decile ranking, and your LSAT score. Your undergraduate GPA was the most influential positive factor, pulling the prediction toward success because it's close to the typical 3.21 average for students who pass the bar. Similarly, your Year 3 decile of 4 worked in your favor compared to lower performers, nudging the model toward expecting you to pass. However, your LSAT score of 32—below the 36.7 average—had the biggest negative pull, significantly raising the model's concern about bar failure risk despite your full-time student status, which is common for 92.7% of applicants. Overall, while your GPA and decile rankings helped balance things out, the LSAT gap tipped the scales against you relative to students with stronger standardized test results who get approved. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer, but a data-driven call to protect the school's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, strengthening your GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your resilience and any bar prep experience. Many applicants in similar situations succeed by addressing these key areas head-on.
